Tafseer:
A specific warning within 'Al-Qari'ah': This is one of the names of the Resurrection. It means 'the striking one' or 'the knocking one'. 'Qara'a-l-bab' means: he knocked at the door; this name points to the aspect of the Resurrection that—just as a night-comer knocks at the door, startling those sleeping—will manifest suddenly. No one knows its time. It will appear suddenly and cause a major upheaval. This name warns that since it is the greatest upheaval and its time is unknown, safety lies in remaining alert.
The style of expression here is like an alarm, preparing all listeners to hear the subject. The braying of the name itself startles the listener, representing the panic of that Day. A similar style appeared in Surah Al-Haqqah, and we explained it. (Footnote 1)
_____
Footnote 1: Refer to Tadabbur-i-Qur'an, Vol. 7, pp. 541-542.
This question increases the gravity of the alarm: those who are untroubled, regarding it as a minor thing, should pay attention, hear its state, and prepare for it.

Rebuking their heedlessness: This style was explained in various places. Along with the question, it expresses regret over their heedlessness, as if to say: you cannot comprehend what this sudden calamity is, and what will befall those who are mocking it despite repeated warnings.

No one will help another on that Day: This describes the situation on that Day: people will be like scattered moths. No one will have his family, tribe, or associates, nor those assumed intercessors upon whom they relied. Rather, everyone will emerge alone and face only his deeds, as stated in Surah Az-Zalzalah:
(That Day, mankind will depart separated, to be shown their deeds) (Az-Zalzalah, 99:6),
and in Surah Al-Mu'minun:
(So when the Trumpet is blown, no relationship will there be among them that Day, nor will they ask about one another. And those whose scales are heavy [with good results]—it is they who are the successful. But those whose scales are light—those are the ones who have lost their souls, [abiding] in Hell eternally.) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:101-103).
The distress that will affect everyone on that Day is depicted in Surah Al-Ma'arij as:
(And no friend will ask of a friend, though they will be made to see each other. The criminal will wish that he could be ransomed from the punishment of that Day by his children, and his wife and his brother, and his nearest kindred who sheltered him, and whoever is on earth entirely, then it would save him.) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:10-14).

All strongholds will be devastated: Just like family ties, all fortresses, strongholds, and buildings will be devastated on that Day. Even the mountains will become like carded wool—meaning their particles will be dispersed.
The mountains are mentioned specifically because the deniers mocked, asking: 'When the Resurrection comes, will it uproot the mountains?' This question and its answer are recorded in the Qur'an.
'Ihn' refers to wool that has been dyed and prepared for spinning. Since its fibers are completely separated, it is used as a metaphor here. The comparison is to show the dispersion, not the color.

Only righteous deeds will benefit on that Day: On that Day, the only beneficial thing will be a person's righteous deeds. Everyone's deeds will be weighed. Whoever's scales are heavy will succeed, and whoever's scales are light will fail. The scale on that Day will be a specific scale of justice, as stated in Surah Al-Anbiya:
(And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection) (Al-Anbiya, 21:47).
The characteristic of this scale on that Day is described in Surah Al-A'raf as: the weight will be only in the truth; falsehood will possess no weight at all. This scale will weigh everyone's deeds and show who possesses truth and weight, and whose deeds are worthless and hollow, as stated:
(And the weighing [of deeds] that Day will be the truth. So those whose scales are heavy—it is they who will be the successful. And those whose scales are light—they are the ones who will have lost their souls for what they used to do of injustice to Our verses.) (Al-A'raf, 7:8-9).

'He will be in a pleasant life' (fahuwa fi 'ishatin-radiyah) - Although the pronoun is singular because of 'man' (whoever), it can represent a plural meaning, as indicated in the verses of Surah Al-A'raf cited above. It is stated: they will be in a pleasant life—meaning they will have whatever they desire, and their Lord will bless them with even more.

This describes the end of those who possess only falsehood, with no truth in their records, or whose minor good deeds were nullified by their beliefs and motives. It is stated: their home will be an abyss. This is explained further as: 'A burning Fire' (narun hamiyah)—meaning the pit of Hell.
'Umm' means mother, but here it is used metaphorically for a home and refuge, which is highly eloquent.
In 'ma hiyah' (what it is), the 'ha' is the 'ha' of pause, added to maintain the rhyme, as has appeared previously.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8, 9, 10, and 11 was combined in the original text.]
